content:
Why the ultimate technology of abundance is creating an economy of artificial waste.

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I. The Tragedy of the Peacock’s Tail
In evolutionary biology, a male peacock carries a massive, heavy, energy-draining tail. It serves no functional purpose for survival; it actually makes it harder to escape predators. It exists purely as a signal to outcompete other peacocks. If every peacock could collectively agree to shrink their tails by 90%, they would all be safer, healthier, and more efficient.
But they cannot trust each other to do it. The moment one peacock grows a slightly larger feather, the arms race resets. Defection from the race means genetic erasure. This is the Prisoner’s Dilemma in its purest, most destructive form: a structural trap where individual rational behavior leads to a collectively catastrophic outcome.
Throughout economic history, certain technologies have acted less like engines of progress and more like a Black Plague (or peacock’s tail). These are innovations that do not expand the economic pie; they merely automate and accelerate the fighting over how the existing slices are divided. They scale efficiency, but they destroy scarcity. Once a single player introduces a “distributive disease” into a free market, there is no turning back. Every competitor must adopt it immediately, taking on massive, permanent overhead costs just to stay exactly in the same place.
We were promised that Artificial Intelligence would usher in a post-scarcity utopia of human leisure and scientific breakthroughs. Instead, AI is acting as a universal amplifier for these exact zero-sum traps. It is lowering the cost of generating corporate noise to near-zero, forcing the rest of society to spend trillions just to filter it out. We are running as fast as we can just to stay still.
II. The Archetypal Disease: Modern Marketing
The most obvious example of an economic tumor is the modern advertising industry. From a macroeconomic standpoint, a massive percentage of marketing is pure, distributive waste. If Company A spends $5 billion on ads and Company B spends $5 billion on ads, their market shares often stay exactly at 50/50. Billions of dollars, alongside the minds of our brightest data scientists, psychologists, and creatives, are vaporized just to maintain a fragile status quo.
Marketing is business’s original Black Plague. You cannot unilaterally choose to stop advertising; if you do, your brand is instantly erased from public consciousness.
Now, introduce Artificial Intelligence. When AI tools were exclusive, early adopters enjoyed massive, temporary profit margins. But as access democratizes, that competitive advantage completely evaporates, leaving the entire market trapped in a permanent “Red Queen’s Race.”
First, it triggers the death of premium pricing. When any agency can generate a flawless, hyper-targeted ad campaign in 30 seconds, clients refuse to pay human labor prices. A race to the bottom ensues, shrinking the industry’s total revenue.
Second, it institutes a permanent AI tax. Even though revenues fall back to baseline levels, corporate overhead remains permanently higher. Companies become entirely dependent on paying continuous “compute fees” to cloud tech giants just to keep their automated software running.
Finally, it causes a hyper-inflation of attention. Because AI allows everyone to spin up thousands of ad variants instantly, the internet becomes flooded with automated content. But human attention remains strictly finite. As the digital space jams up, the cost to acquire a single customer (CAC) skyrockets. The advertising industry won’t get richer; their clients will simply demand ten times the volume of work for a fraction of the cost, while infrastructure monopolies collect a permanent toll on a depleted sector.
III. Spreading the Infection
The tragedy of AI is that it isn’t just breaking marketing; it is introducing this exact, zero-sum Black Plague across entirely different sectors of the human economy simultaneously.
1. The Litigation Epidemic (Automated Bureaucracy)
Legal systems are meant to protect rights, but corporate law frequently operates as a defensive tax. AI can now draft flawless, highly complex 50-page legal briefs, compliance forms, or patent applications in seconds. Large corporations are already weaponizing this to launch automated, low-cost legal attacks — such as filing thousands of hyper-specific patent applications at once to completely lock up an industry domain.
Because competing legal teams and regulatory courts cannot read this tidal wave of documents with human eyes, they are forced to buy their own AI systems to analyze and counter the filings. We are rapidly entering an era where corporate AI lawyers are suing other corporate AI lawyers, generating trillions of pages of documents that no human will ever read, permanently gridlocking human justice.
2. The Code Inflation Epidemic (Software Bloat)
Software development used to be bottlenecked by human hours. With AI coding assistants, engineers can now generate thousands of lines of code in a single keystroke. This has triggered a massive wave of “Code Bloat.” Instead of writing elegant, efficient software, companies are flooding their systems with massive, poorly understood, AI-generated code bases.
To manage this explosion of sloppy code, companies must deploy more AI tools to review, debug, and patch the system. Security teams are trapped using AI to scan for vulnerabilities that were introduced by another AI. The software industry is spending billions just to maintain basic stability, while the actual end-user experience stagnates.
3. The Homework Arms Race (Automated Credentialism)
Education is highly productive when it teaches skills, but it becomes a disease when it transforms into a pure sorting mechanism to filter job applications. AI has turned this sorting mechanism into a completely circular loop. A student uses AI to write an essay in five seconds; the teacher uses an AI detector to grade it; the student uses an AI “humanizer” to bypass the detector. Zero learning occurs, yet massive energy and capital are spent.
Because basic benchmarks are now automated, employers are forced to raise the barrier to entry even higher. Young people are forced to acquire an absurd number of extra credentials, certificates, and advanced degrees just to prove they are capable of entry-level roles, driving them into deeper debt and longer schooling without increasing their actual capabilities.
4. The Synthetic Trust Tax (Cybersecurity Overdrive)
Historically, verifying that a document, an image, or a voice recording was real required basic institutional checks. AI has made the forging of digital reality free, immediate, and indistinguishable from truth. The rise of hyper-realistic deepfakes and automated, hyper-personalized phishing scams has completely destroyed baseline digital trust.
As a result, every bank, hospital, utility provider, and government agency must now pay a permanent, massive “Trust Tax” — spending billions annually on advanced AI cybersecurity systems just to prove that their clients, employees, and data are actually real. This money does not make a hospital better at healing patients or a bank better at saving money; it is a purely defensive, structural cost just to keep the lights on.
IV. The Compute and Energy Tollbooth
When we step back and analyze this interconnected web of AI-driven diseases, a terrifying macroeconomic realization emerges: the game is rigged so that the house always wins.
In a traditional economy, hyper-efficiency lowers prices for consumers and expands corporate profit margins. In an AI-driven Red Queen’s Race, the financial value generated by automation does not stay with the companies doing the work, nor does it trickle down to consumers. Instead, it is extracted entirely by a small cartel of infrastructure monopolies.
The Circular Extraction of AI Value

• The Weapon: AI Content, Code, and Lawsuits are generated at scale.

• The Shield: AI Filters, Defenses, and Security are deployed to block them.

• The Toll: Both sides rely on massive computing power, funneling all economic value back to the infrastructure source.
Whether an advertising agency is using AI to generate ten thousand automated ad variants, or a platform is using AI to filter out that synthetic spam, both sides of the transaction must pay a permanent toll to the entities providing the computing power. Every automated lawsuit filed, every line of code bloated, and every deepfake generated requires massive amounts of advanced graphic processing units (GPUs) and astronomical volumes of electricity.
The ultimate winners of the AI revolution are not the creators, the visionaries, or the workers. The winners are the cloud computing giants hosting the models and the utility monopolies providing the power grid. AI has not liberated human labor; it has simply instituted a permanent Compute Tax on the foundational plumbing of society.
V. Breaking the Trap
How does society escape a Prisoner’s Dilemma when defection means financial or operational ruin? History teaches us that you cannot simply ask corporations to act ethically; you must rewrite the structural rules of the economic sandbox itself. To turn the Black Plague of AI back into the productive tool it should be, humanity could deploy three structural overhauls:
1. Transitioning from the “Ad Model” to Micro-Payments
The modern internet is a breeding ground for AI noise because its underlying business model relies entirely on human attention data. If content is free, volume is king — and AI can produce infinite volume. Shifting the digital architecture toward frictionless micro-payments changes the entire landscape. If accessing a web search, an article, or an app costs a fraction of a cent rather than an ad impression, the incentive to flood the internet with low-quality AI content disappears overnight. When the financial reward for harvesting eyeballs is removed, the automated spam arms race collapses.
2. Legislative “Friction Taxes” on Automated Generation
To prevent corporate AI systems from completely gridlocking our legal, regulatory, and administrative systems, governments must introduce artificial friction. This means implementing a “Compute Tax” or processing fee applied specifically to automated, bulk-generated document filings. If filing a patent application costs a human $500, filing ten thousand AI-generated patent variants should carry an exponentially scaling financial penalty. By making mass synthetic generation economically unviable, we can protect human infrastructure from automated suffocation.
3. The Rise of “Proof-of-Human” Safe Havens
As synthetic reality becomes cheaper and more perfect, the premium value in society will paradoxically shift back to un-automatable human verification. We must see the creation of legally protected, human-only digital and physical zones. This includes strict cryptographic verification systems — like decentralized digital signatures linked to biological identity — to guarantee that code, emails, or legal documents were crafted by a human being. Just as the industrial revolution gave rise to a premium market for “hand-made” goods, the AI revolution will force the economy to carve out “human-made” sectors where AI automation is legally barred from competing.
VI. Reclaiming the Utopia
Artificial Intelligence represents one of the greatest technological achievements in human history, possessing the genuine capability to decode complex proteins, optimize clean energy distribution, and accelerate medical breakthroughs. Yet, if left unchecked within an unregulated free market, its potential will be entirely swallowed by zero-sum distributive waste. We will find ourselves trapped in a hyper-automated nightmare: writing code to patch code, filing lawsuits to block lawsuits, and generating ads to fight ads — all while b
